name: Yomi (Underworld)
shortDescription: Yomi is the land of the dead in Japanese mythology, a dark and gloomy realm ruled over by Izanami-no-Mikoto. It is a place of decay and impurity, sharply contrasting with the upper world.
description: Yomi, also known as Yomi-no-kuni or Ne-no-kuni, is the Japanese underworld, a realm of perpetual darkness and decay where the dead reside. In Shinto mythology, it is described as a grim and polluted land, a stark contrast to the vibrant and pure world of the living. The entrance to Yomi is often depicted as being located in Izumo Province. The most well-known myth associated with Yomi is the story of Izanagi and Izanami. After Izanami died giving birth to the fire god Kagutsuchi, Izanagi descended into Yomi to retrieve her. He found her transformed into a decaying corpse, infested with maggots and monstrous forms. Horrified by what he saw, Izanagi fled, sealing the entrance to Yomi with a large boulder. This act led to the eternal separation of the realms of the living and the dead, and to Izanami becoming the ruler of Yomi and the goddess of death. The concept of Yomi highlights the Shinto emphasis on purity and the fear of death and decay. It is a place of impurity (kegare) that must be avoided. The narrative also explains the origin of death and the separation between the divine and human realms. Yomi shares thematic similarities with other underworlds in various mythologies, such as the Greek Hades or the Mesopotamian Kur, though its specific characteristics and role within the Shinto creation myth give it a unique place in Japanese cosmology. The story of Izanagi's failed attempt to bring Izanami back also resonates with the Orpheus and Eurydice myth, highlighting the universal themes of love, loss, and the irreversibility of death.
